extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: {'type': 'Lab-scale gantry crane', 'main_components': [{'component': 'Physical crane', 'description': 'A mechanical gantry crane system designed for laboratory-scale operations, likely featuring a movable bridge, hoist, and trolley mechanism for lifting and transporting loads.'}, {'component': 'Controller', 'description': 'Embedded or programmable logic controller (PLC) responsible for managing the crane's actuators (motors, drives) and ensuring precise movement and operational safety.'}], 'operational_domain': {'environment': 'Indoor laboratory setting', 'use_case': 'Demonstration of digital twin concepts, validation of control algorithms, fault detection, and continuous validation methods', 'scale': 'Small-scale (lab-scale) for reproducibility and educational purposes'}, 'key_characteristics': [{'characteristic': 'Modularity', 'description': 'The system is designed to be easily integrated with digital twin components, facilitating bidirectional data exchange.'}, {'characteristic': 'Reproducibility', 'description': 'Publicly available and relies on open-source or freely available software, making it accessible for research and education.'}, {'characteristic': 'Cyber-Physical System (CPS) compatibility', 'description': 'The physical system is part of a cyber-physical system, enabling real-time interaction between the physical and digital domains.'}, {'characteristic': 'Scalability', 'description': 'While lab-scale, the system exemplifies principles applicable to larger industrial gantry cranes or similar systems.'}]}

extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system exemplars (GreenhouseDT, Air Quality Management, and the lab-scale gantry crane) primarily adopt **decentralized or ad-hoc architectures** rather than centralized frameworks. The **GreenhouseDT** and **Air Quality Management** exemplars leverage **single-board computers (e.g., Raspberry Pi)** for localized data acquisition and processing, implying **multiple digital twin instances** may operate independently or in loosely coupled configurations. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar explicitly uses **AutomationML-based digital twins**, suggesting a **hierarchical or modular structure** where the digital twin may represent discrete subsystems (e.g., sensor networks, simulation modules) with **coordination mechanisms** managed via **Microsoft Azure tools**. The **GreenhouseDT** employs **virtual machines with prerecorded data**, indicating potential **time-synchronized batch processing** for behavioral adaptation, though no explicit synchronization frequency is documented. The **gantry crane system** (logistics/cyber-physical domain) aligns with this pattern, using **containerization** for modular deployment, implying **scalable multiplicities** without centralized orchestration. No explicit latency requirements or real-time constraints are specified in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system exemplars demonstrate an **ad-hoc, component-based orchestration** rather than a standardized constellation. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplifies a **self-adaptive architecture** where both the physical greenhouse and its digital twin exhibit **behavioral adaptation**, coordinated via **InfluxDB (time-series database)** and **ActiveMQ (message broker)** for event-driven communication. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar uses a **pipeline architecture** on **Microsoft Azure**, integrating **runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction** services with **AutomationML** for model-driven engineering. The **gantry crane system** (logistics domain) employs the **C4 Model** for architecture visualization, suggesting a **containerized microservices approach** with **modular components** (e.g., control, sensing, actuation) but lacks explicit orchestration frameworks. All exemplars avoid commercial digital twin frameworks, opting for **custom integration patterns** (e.g., virtualization, containerization) to ensure **easy dissemination** and **domain-specific adaptability**. Resource management is implicit, relying on **single-board computers (Raspberry Pi)** and **cloud/VM-based services** for scalability.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin systems exhibit **limited horizontal integration** with external systems, primarily relying on **domain-specific technologies** and **ad-hoc protocols**. The **GreenhouseDT** integrates with **InfluxDB (time-series database)** and **ActiveMQ (message broker)** for internal data exchange but lacks explicit interoperability standards for external systems. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar uses **AutomationML** for digital twin modeling and **Microsoft Azure tools** for pipeline implementation, implying **cloud-native integration** but no standardized protocols for enterprise systems. The **gantry crane system** (logistics domain) does not document external integrations, though its **C4 Model-based architecture** suggests potential for **modular extensions** via containerization. No evidence of **OPC UA, MQTT, or other industrial interoperability standards** is provided. Dependencies are localized to **single-board computers, virtual machines, or cloud services**, with no mention of **cross-Digital Twin or enterprise system integration patterns**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system employs **behavioral adaptation models** for both the physical system (e.g., greenhouse, air quality management) and its digital twin counterpart, enabling dynamic adjustments in real-time. In the **GreenhouseDT** exemplar, the digital twin leverages **prerecorded data** embedded in virtual machine images for simulation and validation, while relying on an **InfluxDB** time-series database for structured data storage and retrieval. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar utilizes **AutomationML-based digital twin models** for geometric and behavioral representation, integrating with Microsoft Azure tools for pipeline execution. The models support **runtime visualization, simulation, and predictive analytics** for indoor air quality management, with a focus on virus spread prevention. Data sources include **sensor inputs** (e.g., Raspberry Pi-based sensors) and **historical/prerecorded datasets** for training and validation. Model relationships are implicitly defined through **message brokering (ActiveMQ)** for communication between components, ensuring synchronization between physical and digital domains. The **lab-scale gantry crane** exemplar, while not detailed in model specifics, aligns with the broader trend of **ad-hoc model-driven approaches** using containerization for dissemination, suggesting modular and potentially composable model architectures.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The **Air Quality Management** exemplar features a **Raspberry Pi** equipped with a **set of sensors** for real-time environmental monitoring, including parameters relevant to indoor air quality (e.g., CO₂, particulate matter, humidity, temperature). The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar does not explicitly detail sensor specifications but implies integration with physical systems via **prerecorded data** and **InfluxDB** for data ingestion. No explicit mention of **sampling frequencies, spatial distribution, or transmission rates** is provided, though the use of **ActiveMQ** suggests a **message-based data collection mechanism** for sensor data aggregation. The **lab-scale gantry crane** exemplar lacks specific sensor details but aligns with broader cyber-physical systems (CPS) trends, where sensors would likely include **positional, motion, and load sensors** for container tracking and crane operation.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar emphasizes **self-adaptive behavioral fidelity**, where both the physical greenhouse and its digital twin dynamically adjust based on real-time or prerecorded data. Validation is implied through **prerecorded datasets** in virtual machine images, ensuring reproducibility. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar focuses on **model-driven engineering fidelity**, with **AutomationML-based models** validated via **runtime visualization, simulation, and predictive analytics** for virus spread prevention. Microsoft Azure tools facilitate **data-driven validation**, though explicit methods (e.g., uncertainty quantification, error correction) are not detailed. The **lab-scale gantry crane** exemplar lacks specific fidelity metrics but adheres to **ad-hoc validation approaches**, likely relying on **containerized deployment** for consistency. Uncertainty sources (e.g., sensor noise, model approximations) are not quantified, and no explicit **quality assurance processes** (e.g., calibration protocols, cross-validation) are described in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The data flow from the **Physical Crane** to the digital twin is facilitated through the **Physical Crane Interface**, a Python program that connects to the crane's controllers. The **Logging Application** continuously captures and transmits the crane's state data (e.g., position, velocity, acceleration, and operational status) to the **TimescaleDB database** in real-time. Data types transmitted include kinematic measurements (e.g., puck coordinates, crane joint angles, and trajectory execution metrics) and operational logs (e.g., controller commands, error states, and timestamps). The communication protocol is not explicitly specified, but the system implies a direct Python-based interface for data acquisition. Transmission frequency is continuous, triggered by state changes or predefined polling intervals (not quantified in the document). Data quality assurance is implicit through the **Continuous Validation Service**, which cross-references physical measurements with simulated trajectories to detect anomalies. Preprocessing steps are not detailed, but the **Trajectory Generator Service** and **Simulation Service** rely on structured kinematic models, suggesting data normalization or filtering may occur before storage. Data integration is centralized via the **TimescaleDB database**, which serves as the single source of truth for all digital twin services, including the **Dashboard** and **Continuous Validation Service**.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control flows from the digital twin to the **Physical Crane** are mediated by the **Control Application**, a Python program that interfaces with the **Physical Crane Interface**. Commands generated by the **Trajectory Generator Service** (e.g., optimal trajectories with zero residual swinging) are transmitted as structured control messages to the crane's controllers. The exact message format is unspecified, but the system implies a deterministic, kinematics-driven command structure. Validation and authentication mechanisms are not explicitly described, though the **Continuous Validation Service** ensures executed trajectories adhere to simulated models, indirectly validating command integrity. Feedback systems are implicit: the **Logging Application** captures real-time crane state data, which is compared against simulated trajectories to detect deviations. Conflict resolution is not detailed, but the **MQTT Broker** facilitates signaling between services, suggesting asynchronous event handling for command prioritization or error recovery. Command execution confirmation is implied via the **Dashboard**, which notifies operators of threshold breaches (e.g., trajectory deviations exceeding acceptable limits).
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The digital twin’s technical network infrastructure relies on a **TimescaleDB database** (a PostgreSQL extension optimized for time-series data) as the central data repository, connected to services via unspecified protocols. The **MQTT Broker** enables lightweight, publish-subscribe communication between services (e.g., **Trajectory Generator Service**, **Simulation Service**, and **Continuous Validation Service**), ensuring low-latency signaling. Network topology is not explicitly defined, but the system’s modular design (C4 container abstraction) suggests a distributed architecture with services communicating over a local or dedicated network. Bandwidth and latency requirements are not quantified, though real-time kinematic data (e.g., trajectory metrics) imply low-latency constraints. Reliability and redundancy mechanisms are not detailed, but the **Continuous Validation Service**’s threshold-based alerts imply fault detection. Security protocols are unspecified, though the use of a dedicated **MQTT Broker** and centralized database suggests controlled access points.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The digital twin’s deployment infrastructure is not explicitly described as cloud/edge/on-premise, but the system’s components (e.g., **TimescaleDB**, **Grafana Dashboard**, and Python services) suggest an **on-premise or edge deployment** due to the lab-scale, real-time nature of the gantry crane operations. Computational requirements are moderate: the **Trajectory Generator Service** and **Simulation Service** rely on kinematic models, while the **Logging Application** and **Continuous Validation Service** require continuous data processing. Storage demands are driven by the **TimescaleDB database**, which archives time-series crane state data. Scalability mechanisms are not mentioned, but the modular C4 architecture implies potential horizontal scaling for services like the **MQTT Broker** or **Dashboard**. Deployment models are not specified, though the Python-based services and TimescaleDB suggest containerization (e.g., Docker) or virtualization. Availability/reliability assurance is implied by the **Continuous Validation Service**’s real-time monitoring and operator alerts, but no explicit redundancy (e.g., failover) is described.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Digital Twin exemplars (GreenhouseDT and Air Quality Management) employ an **ad-hoc development approach** rather than relying on commercial digital twin frameworks. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar leverages **virtual machines (VMs) with pre-recorded data** for ease of installation, while the **Air Quality Management** exemplar uses **AutomationML** for digital twin modeling and **Microsoft Azure tools** for pipeline implementation. Both exemplars utilize **containerization or virtualization technologies** (e.g., VMs, Raspberry Pi-based systems) to facilitate dissemination and reproducibility. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar includes **three distinct applications** (runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction), suggesting a modular lifecycle where the digital twin evolves through iterative development phases. **Version control and governance** are implied via GitHub hosting for documentation and code, but explicit methodologies (e.g., Agile, DevOps) are not detailed in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The **Air Quality Management** exemplar demonstrates **decision support capabilities** through three core applications: **runtime visualization** (real-time monitoring of indoor air quality), **simulation** (modeling air flow and pollutant dispersion), and **prediction** (forecasting virus spread risks). The digital twin pipeline leverages **Microsoft Azure tools**, implying cloud-based analytics and processing. **User interaction** is facilitated via runtime visualization interfaces, though specific methods (e.g., dashboards, alerts) are not detailed. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar focuses on **self-adaptive behavior**, suggesting dynamic adjustments to both the physical greenhouse and its digital twin based on real-time sensor data (InfluxDB database) and message brokering (ActiveMQ). **Confidence measures** for recommendations are not explicitly quantified, but the use of **pre-recorded data in VMs** and **sensor-driven feedback loops** implies empirical validation. No explicit mention of machine learning or statistical models is provided.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The **Air Quality Management** exemplar adheres to **AutomationML** for digital twin modeling, a standardized format for engineering data exchange. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar uses **InfluxDB** (a time-series database standard) and **ActiveMQ** (a widely adopted message broker protocol), though these are not domain-specific standards. Neither exemplar explicitly mentions **certification requirements** or **compliance frameworks** (e.g., ISO, IEC). **Interoperability** is implied through the use of **Raspberry Pi sensors** (common IoT hardware) and **containerization/virtualization** (e.g., VMs, Azure tools), but no formal standards (e.g., OPC UA, MTConnect) are referenced. The **ad-hoc approach** of the exemplars suggests limited adherence to proprietary or industry-specific digital twin standards.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil
block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give
block_5_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more

opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.
